1→# 07 — MPP: Machine Payment Protocol
     2→
     3→> MPP (Machine Payment Protocol) provides session-based streaming payments for agent
     4→> operations. While x402 handles per-request micropayments, MPP handles pre-funded sessions
     5→> where an agent deposits once and draws against a balance across many requests. This
     6→> document specifies the protocol mechanics, session lifecycle, budget delegation via SPTs,
     7→> and multi-rail payment support.
     8→
     9→
    10→> **Implementation**: Deferred
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## 1. Protocol Stack
    15→
    16→Three payment protocols operate at different scales. MPP occupies the middle layer:
    17→
    18→```
    19→┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    20→│  ERC-8183 Escrow (Trustless Job Payments)    │
    21→│  $10-$500 per job. On-chain deposit.         │
    22→│  Milestone-based release. 48h dispute window.│
    23→├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    24→│  MPP Sessions (Pre-Funded Streaming)         │  ← This document
    25→│  $5-$50 per session. Off-chain vouchers.     │
    26→│  Low per-request overhead. 1h default TTL.   │
    27→├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    28→│  x402 (Per-Request Micropayments)            │
    29→│  $0.001-$1 per request. No session state.    │
    30→│  ERC-3009 signed authorization. Stateless.   │
    31→└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    32→```
    33→
    34→### 1.1 When to Use MPP vs. x402 vs. ERC-8183
    35→
    36→| Scenario | Protocol | Why |
    37→|---|---|---|
    38→| Single inference call | x402 | No session overhead for one-off requests |
    39→| Draft iteration (5-20 calls) | x402 | Each call is independent; total < $1 |
    40→| Full build run (50-200 calls) | MPP | Session avoids re-signing each request |
    41→| Trustless job between strangers | ERC-8183 | On-chain escrow protects both parties |
    42→| Long-running agent operation | MPP | Pre-funded session with auto-extend |
    43→| Mid-build scope change | x402 | One-off top-up via independent payment |
    44→
    45→### 1.2 Composability
    46→
    47→The three protocols compose naturally in a typical workflow:
    48→
    49→```
    50→1. DRAFTING: x402 per-request ($0.03/call)
    51→   → User iterates on idea, accumulates context
    52→   → Total: $0.45 for 15 draft iterations
    53→
    54→2. PROPOSAL: x402 ($0.05 one-off)
    55→   → Agent generates formal proposal with cost estimate
    56→
    57→3. BUILD: MPP session ($20 deposit)
    58→   → User opens session, agent draws per-request
    59→   → 150 requests over 90 minutes
    60→   → Total drawn: $14.30
    61→
    62→4. ADJUSTMENT: x402 ($5 one-off)
    63→   → Mid-build scope change, independent payment
    64→
    65→5. SETTLEMENT: MPP session close
    66→   → $5.70 refunded (unspent balance)
    67→```
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## 2. Session Lifecycle
    72→
    73→### 2.1 Session Open
    74→
    75→```
    76→POST /v1/mpp/sessions
    77→Body: {
    78→  "authorization": {
    79→    "from": "0xClientWallet",
    80→    "to": "0xOperatorWallet",
    81→    "value": "10000000",              // $10.00 USDC (6 decimals)
    82→    "valid_after": "0",
    83→    "valid_before": "1711238167",
    84→    "nonce": "0x...",
    85→    "v": 28, "r": "0x...", "s": "0x..."
    86→  }
    87→}
    88→
    89→Response: {
    90→  "session_id": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
    91→  "funded_amount": "10000000",        // $10.00
    92→  "expires_at": 1711238167,           // TTL: 1 hour default
    93→  "status": "active"
    94→}
    95→```
    96→
    97→The gateway verifies the ERC-3009 signature off-chain (no RPC call needed), creates the
    98→session in a DashMap (in-memory) backed by SQLite (persistent), and returns the session ID.
    99→
   100→### 2.2 Session Draw
   101→
   102→Subsequent requests include an `X-Payment` header with session credentials:
   103→
   104→```
   105→POST /v1/messages
   106→X-Payment: {"intent": "session", "session_id": "a1b2c3d4...", "session_op": "draw"}
   107→Body: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "messages": [...] }
   108→
   109→Response headers:
   110→  X-Roko-Cost: 0.0234
   111→  X-Roko-Session-Balance: 9.9766
   112→  X-Roko-Session-Draws: 1
   113→  Payment-Receipt: {"receipt_id":"...","amount_charged":"23400"}
   114→```
   115→
   116→No new ERC-3009 signature needed per request. The gateway checks session balance, runs
   117→the handler, computes actual cost from token usage, and deducts from session balance.
   118→
   119→### 2.3 Session Top-Up
   120→
   121→```
   122→POST /v1/mpp/sessions/{id}/top-up
   123→Body: {
   124→  "authorization": { ... ERC-3009 ... "value": "5000000" }  // +$5.00
   125→}
   126→
   127→Response: {
   128→  "session_id": "a1b2c3d4...",
   129→  "previous_balance": "2340000",     // $2.34
   130→  "added": "5000000",                // $5.00
   131→  "new_balance": "7340000"           // $7.34
   132→}
   133→```
   134→
   135→### 2.4 Session Close
   136→
   137→```
   138→DELETE /v1/mpp/sessions/{id}
   139→
   140→Response: {
   141→  "session_id": "a1b2c3d4...",
   142→  "total_funded": "15000000",        // $15.00 (original + top-up)
   143→  "total_drawn": "12250000",         // $12.25
   144→  "refund_amount": "2750000",        // $2.75
   145→  "draw_count": 47,
   146→  "status": "settled"
   147→}
   148→```
   149→
   150→The operator batches the on-chain settlement: drawn funds transfer to the operator,
   151→refund to the client. Single on-chain transaction.
   152→
   153→### 2.5 Session States
   154→
   155→```
   156→Active → Exhausted → Active (via top-up)
   157→                  ↘ Expired (TTL elapsed)
   158→                  ↘ Settled (explicitly closed)
   159→
   160→States:
   161→  Active:    accepting draws
   162→  Exhausted: balance = 0, can be revived via top-up
   163→  Expired:   TTL elapsed, no more draws
   164→  Settled:   closed, funds distributed
   165→```
   166→
   167→A background task calls `expire_stale()` periodically to transition timed-out sessions.
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→## 3. Shared Payment Tokens (SPTs)
   172→
   173→SPTs enable budget delegation — the orchestrator issues scoped authorizations to
   174→sub-agents:
   175→
   176→### 3.1 SPT Structure
   177→
   178→```rust
   179→/// Shared Payment Token — a scoped, time-limited authorization
   180→/// for a sub-agent to spend from the orchestrator's budget.
   181→pub struct SharedPaymentToken {
   182→    /// Unique identifier for this SPT.
   183→    pub spt_id: Uuid,
   184→
   185→    /// Parent session this SPT draws from.
   186→    pub parent_session_id: Uuid,
   187→
   188→    /// Maximum amount this SPT can spend (USDC base units).
   189→    pub max_amount: u64,
   190→
   191→    /// Expiry timestamp (Unix seconds).
   192→    pub expires_at: u64,
   193→
   194→    /// Services this SPT is scoped to.
   195→    /// The sub-agent can only spend at these service endpoints.
   196→    pub scoped_to: Vec<ServiceEndpoint>,
   197→
   198→    /// Current amount drawn against this SPT.
   199→    pub drawn: u64,
   200→
   201→    /// Agent that holds this SPT.
   202→    pub holder: AgentId,
   203→}
   204→```
   205→
   206→### 3.2 Budget Delegation Example
   207→
   208→```
   209→Orchestrator budget: $15.25 (MPP session)
   210→  ├── Implementer SPT: $8.00 max
   211→  │   scoped_to: [inference_gateway, mcp_tools]
   212→  │   expires_at: 2h from now
   213→  │
   214→  ├── Reviewer SPT: $3.00 max
   215→  │   scoped_to: [inference_gateway]
   216→  │   expires_at: 2h from now
   217→  │
   218→  ├── AutoFixer SPT: $2.00 max
   219→  │   scoped_to: [inference_gateway]
   220→  │   expires_at: 2h from now
   221→  │
   222→  └── Reserve: $2.25 (held by conductor)
   223→      No SPT issued — conductor allocates on demand
   224→```
   225→
   226→### 3.3 SPT Constraints
   227→
   228→| Constraint | Value | Rationale |
   229→|---|---|---|
   230→| `max_amount` | Hard ceiling | Prevents runaway spending |
   231→| `expires_at` | Typically 2-4h | Limits exposure window |
   232→| `scoped_to` | Service endpoint list | Prevents unauthorized service use |
   233→| `max_sub_agent_budget_pct` | 60% of total | No single agent gets majority |
   234→| `spt_expiry_hours` | 4h default | Configurable in `roko.toml` |
   235→
   236→### 3.4 SPT Exhaustion
   237→
   238→When a sub-agent exhausts its SPT:
   239→
   240→1. Sub-agent stops and reports to the conductor.
   241→2. Conductor options:
   242→   a. Reallocate from reserve pool.
   243→   b. Downgrade model tier (Opus → Sonnet, ~50% cost reduction).
   244→   c. Escalate to user for top-up.
   245→3. If no resolution within 5 minutes, the task is paused (not cancelled).
   246→
   247→---
   248→
   249→## 4. Multi-Rail Payment Support
   250→
   251→MPP supports multiple payment rails through the same session interface:
   252→
   253→### 4.1 Supported Rails
   254→
   255→| Rail | Use Case | Settlement |
   256→|---|---|---|
   257→| **USDC on Base** | Primary. Agent-to-agent, x402 compatible | Sub-second on Base L2 |
   258→| **USDC on other L2s** | Cross-chain agents | Bridge + settle |
   259→| **Credit card (Stripe)** | Human buyers, non-crypto-native | Stripe instant payout |
   260→| **Lightning** | Bitcoin-native agents | Sub-second |
   261→| **KORAI** | Korai chain native token | 400ms block time |
   262→
   263→### 4.2 Multi-Rail Session
   264→
   265→A session can accept multiple rails. The operator specifies accepted rails in
   266→configuration:
   267→
   268→```toml
   269→# roko.toml
   270→[billing]
   271→accept_proposals = true
   272→funding_modes = ["escrow", "session", "x402"]
   273→
   274→[billing.session]
   275→accepted_rails = ["usdc_base", "stripe", "korai"]
   276→min_deposit = 5.00
   277→max_session_duration_hours = 24
   278→auto_close_on_idle_minutes = 30
   279→```
   280→
   281→---
   282→
   283→## 5. Cost Transparency
   284→
   285→Every MPP response includes cost transparency headers:
   286→
   287→```
   288→X-Roko-Cost: 0.0234              # What this request actually cost
   289→X-Roko-Naive-Cost: 0.0380        # What it would have cost without caching
   290→X-Roko-Savings: 0.0146           # Cache savings on this request
   291→X-Roko-Cache-Status: semantic-hit # Cache hit type
   292→X-Roko-Provider: anthropic        # Which provider served this request
   293→X-Roko-Tokens-In: 45000
   294→X-Roko-Tokens-Out: 1200
   295→X-Roko-Session-Cost: 4.83         # Cumulative session cost
   296→X-Roko-Session-Savings: 2.32      # Cumulative session savings
   297→X-Roko-Session-Balance: 5.17      # Remaining session balance
   298→```
   299→
   300→Cache hits reduce the provider cost directly, and the client sees the savings. Draft
   301→iterations that hit cache (because the user asks similar questions) cost progressively
   302→less. The system rewards iteration.
   303→
   304→---
   305→
   306→## 6. Multi-Party Revenue Splitting
   307→
   308→When a build involves multiple providers, settlement splits automatically:
   309→
   310→```
   311→User pays $15.25 for a build:
   312→  → $10.20 to inference gateway (LLM calls)
   313→  → $3.10 to compute provider (execution machines)
   314→  → $1.00 to deployment service (DNS, TLS setup)
   315→  → $0.95 to operator (platform margin)
   316→```
   317→
   318→The split happens atomically in a single settlement transaction. For builds that use
   319→marketplace agents (e.g., a security auditor hired from the knowledge marketplace):
   320→
   321→```
   322→  → $2.00 to external security auditor agent
   323→  → $0.20 protocol fee (10% of marketplace transaction)
   324→```
   325→
   326→---
   327→
   328→## 7. The Spread Model
   329→
   330→The gateway's revenue comes from a percentage spread on provider costs:
   331→
   332→```
   333→total_charge = provider_cost × (1.0 + spread_pct)
   334→```
   335→
   336→Default spread: 20%. Reputation tiers reduce the spread:
   337→
   338→| ERC-8004 Tier | Requirements | Spread |
   339→|---|---|---|
   340→| None | New, no history | 20% |
   341→| Basic | 5+ completed builds | 18% |
   342→| Verified | 25+ builds, >90% pass rate | 15% |
   343→| Trusted | 100+ builds, >95% pass rate | 12% |
   344→| Sovereign | 500+ builds | 8% |
   345→
   346→---
   347→
   348→## 8. Refund Mechanics
   349→
   350→### 8.1 Session Refunds
   351→
   352→```
   353→refund_amount = funded_amount - drawn_amount
   354→```
   355→
   356→Unspent balance returns on session close. The operator batches the on-chain transfer.
   357→
   358→### 8.2 Escrow Refunds
   359→
   360→Completed milestones stay paid. Failed milestones refund after the 48-hour dispute window.
   361→Cancelled builds: in-progress milestones refund in full, completed milestones are
   362→irreversible.
   363→
   364→### 8.3 x402 Overpayment
   365→
   366→The pre-authorized value in the ERC-3009 signature is an upper bound. The gateway only
   367→draws actual cost. The difference is never touched (authorization expires).
   368→
   369→---
   370→
   371→## 9. Configuration Reference
   372→
   373→```toml
   374→# roko.toml — billing section
   375→
   376→[billing]
   377→accept_proposals = true
   378→funding_modes = ["escrow", "session", "x402"]
   379→min_proposal_value = 1.00
   380→max_proposal_value = 500.00
   381→operator_margin_pct = 15
   382→
   383→[billing.escrow]
   384→evaluator = "automated"              # automated | peer | manual
   385→release_per_milestone = true
   386→dispute_window_hours = 48
   387→
   388→[billing.session]
   389→min_deposit = 5.00
   390→max_session_duration_hours = 24
   391→auto_close_on_idle_minutes = 30
   392→
   393→[billing.delegation]
   394→max_sub_agent_budget_pct = 60
   395→spt_expiry_hours = 4
   396→```
   397→
   398→---
   399→
   400→## 10. Implementation Status
   401→
   402→> **Implementation status (2026-04-12)**: MPP protocol is fully specified. Session lifecycle
   403→> (open, draw, top-up, close, settle) is designed. SPT budget delegation is specified.
   404→> Multi-rail support is designed. Cost transparency headers are defined. Spread model with
   405→> reputation tiers is defined. Not yet implemented in the Roko runtime. Payment currently
   406→> uses API keys, not x402/MPP.
   407→
   408→---
   409→
   410→## 11. Academic Citations
   411→
   412→- Hammond 2025 — x402 protocol specification (Coinbase / Linux Foundation)
   413→- ERC-3009 — transferWithAuthorization (gasless USDC transfers)
   414→- ERC-8183 — Agent-to-agent task escrow
   415→- Ponton et al. 2024 — Machine Payment Protocol specification
   416→
   417→---
   418→
   419→*Generated from: bardo-backup/tmp/death/14-proposals-and-billing.md (mechanism extracted,
   420→mortality framing removed), bardo-backup/tmp/death/15-cost-tracking.md,
   421→bardo-backup/tmp/death/payments/04-payment-mechanics.md. All naming renames applied:
   422→mori→Roko orchestrator, bardo→Roko, golem→agent.*
   423→

file.content:
# 07 — MPP: Machine Payment Protocol

> MPP (Machine Payment Protocol) provides session-based streaming payments for agent
> operations. While x402 handles per-request micropayments, MPP handles pre-funded sessions
> where an agent deposits once and draws against a balance across many requests. This
> document specifies the protocol mechanics, session lifecycle, budget delegation via SPTs,
> and multi-rail payment support.


> **Implementation**: Deferred

---

## 1. Protocol Stack

Three payment protocols operate at different scales. MPP occupies the middle layer:

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ERC-8183 Escrow (Trustless Job Payments)    │
│  $10-$500 per job. On-chain deposit.         │
│  Milestone-based release. 48h dispute window.│
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  MPP Sessions (Pre-Funded Streaming)         │  ← This document
│  $5-$50 per session. Off-chain vouchers.     │
│  Low per-request overhead. 1h default TTL.   │
├──────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  x402 (Per-Request Micropayments)            │
│  $0.001-$1 per request. No session state.    │
│  ERC-3009 signed authorization. Stateless.   │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 1.1 When to Use MPP vs. x402 vs. ERC-8183

| Scenario | Protocol | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Single inference call | x402 | No session overhead for one-off requests |
| Draft iteration (5-20 calls) | x402 | Each call is independent; total < $1 |
| Full build run (50-200 calls) | MPP | Session avoids re-signing each request |
| Trustless job between strangers | ERC-8183 | On-chain escrow protects both parties |
| Long-running agent operation | MPP | Pre-funded session with auto-extend |
| Mid-build scope change | x402 | One-off top-up via independent payment |

### 1.2 Composability

The three protocols compose naturally in a typical workflow:

```
1. DRAFTING: x402 per-request ($0.03/call)
   → User iterates on idea, accumulates context
   → Total: $0.45 for 15 draft iterations

2. PROPOSAL: x402 ($0.05 one-off)
   → Agent generates formal proposal with cost estimate

3. BUILD: MPP session ($20 deposit)
   → User opens session, agent draws per-request
   → 150 requests over 90 minutes
   → Total drawn: $14.30

4. ADJUSTMENT: x402 ($5 one-off)
   → Mid-build scope change, independent payment

5. SETTLEMENT: MPP session close
   → $5.70 refunded (unspent balance)
```

---

## 2. Session Lifecycle

### 2.1 Session Open

```
POST /v1/mpp/sessions
Body: {
  "authorization": {
    "from": "0xClientWallet",
    "to": "0xOperatorWallet",
    "value": "10000000",              // $10.00 USDC (6 decimals)
    "valid_after": "0",
    "valid_before": "1711238167",
    "nonce": "0x...",
    "v": 28, "r": "0x...", "s": "0x..."
  }
}

Response: {
  "session_id": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
  "funded_amount": "10000000",        // $10.00
  "expires_at": 1711238167,           // TTL: 1 hour default
  "status": "active"
}
```

The gateway verifies the ERC-3009 signature off-chain (no RPC call needed), creates the
session in a DashMap (in-memory) backed by SQLite (persistent), and returns the session ID.

### 2.2 Session Draw

Subsequent requests include an `X-Payment` header with session credentials:

```
POST /v1/messages
X-Payment: {"intent": "session", "session_id": "a1b2c3d4...", "session_op": "draw"}
Body: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "messages": [...] }

Response headers:
  X-Roko-Cost: 0.0234
  X-Roko-Session-Balance: 9.9766
  X-Roko-Session-Draws: 1
  Payment-Receipt: {"receipt_id":"...","amount_charged":"23400"}
```

No new ERC-3009 signature needed per request. The gateway checks session balance, runs
the handler, computes actual cost from token usage, and deducts from session balance.

### 2.3 Session Top-Up

```
POST /v1/mpp/sessions/{id}/top-up
Body: {
  "authorization": { ... ERC-3009 ... "value": "5000000" }  // +$5.00
}

Response: {
  "session_id": "a1b2c3d4...",
  "previous_balance": "2340000",     // $2.34
  "added": "5000000",                // $5.00
  "new_balance": "7340000"           // $7.34
}
```

### 2.4 Session Close

```
DELETE /v1/mpp/sessions/{id}

Response: {
  "session_id": "a1b2c3d4...",
  "total_funded": "15000000",        // $15.00 (original + top-up)
  "total_drawn": "12250000",         // $12.25
  "refund_amount": "2750000",        // $2.75
  "draw_count": 47,
  "status": "settled"
}
```

The operator batches the on-chain settlement: drawn funds transfer to the operator,
refund to the client. Single on-chain transaction.

### 2.5 Session States

```
Active → Exhausted → Active (via top-up)
                  ↘ Expired (TTL elapsed)
                  ↘ Settled (explicitly closed)

States:
  Active:    accepting draws
  Exhausted: balance = 0, can be revived via top-up
  Expired:   TTL elapsed, no more draws
  Settled:   closed, funds distributed
```

A background task calls `expire_stale()` periodically to transition timed-out sessions.

---

## 3. Shared Payment Tokens (SPTs)

SPTs enable budget delegation — the orchestrator issues scoped authorizations to
sub-agents:

### 3.1 SPT Structure

```rust
/// Shared Payment Token — a scoped, time-limited authorization
/// for a sub-agent to spend from the orchestrator's budget.
pub struct SharedPaymentToken {
    /// Unique identifier for this SPT.
    pub spt_id: Uuid,

    /// Parent session this SPT draws from.
    pub parent_session_id: Uuid,

    /// Maximum amount this SPT can spend (USDC base units).
    pub max_amount: u64,

    /// Expiry timestamp (Unix seconds).
    pub expires_at: u64,

    /// Services this SPT is scoped to.
    /// The sub-agent can only spend at these service endpoints.
    pub scoped_to: Vec<ServiceEndpoint>,

    /// Current amount drawn against this SPT.
    pub drawn: u64,

    /// Agent that holds this SPT.
    pub holder: AgentId,
}
```

### 3.2 Budget Delegation Example

```
Orchestrator budget: $15.25 (MPP session)
  ├── Implementer SPT: $8.00 max
  │   scoped_to: [inference_gateway, mcp_tools]
  │   expires_at: 2h from now
  │
  ├── Reviewer SPT: $3.00 max
  │   scoped_to: [inference_gateway]
  │   expires_at: 2h from now
  │
  ├── AutoFixer SPT: $2.00 max
  │   scoped_to: [inference_gateway]
  │   expires_at: 2h from now
  │
  └── Reserve: $2.25 (held by conductor)
      No SPT issued — conductor allocates on demand
```

### 3.3 SPT Constraints

| Constraint | Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| `max_amount` | Hard ceiling | Prevents runaway spending |
| `expires_at` | Typically 2-4h | Limits exposure window |
| `scoped_to` | Service endpoint list | Prevents unauthorized service use |
| `max_sub_agent_budget_pct` | 60% of total | No single agent gets majority |
| `spt_expiry_hours` | 4h default | Configurable in `roko.toml` |

### 3.4 SPT Exhaustion

When a sub-agent exhausts its SPT:

1. Sub-agent stops and reports to the conductor.
2. Conductor options:
   a. Reallocate from reserve pool.
   b. Downgrade model tier (Opus → Sonnet, ~50% cost reduction).
   c. Escalate to user for top-up.
3. If no resolution within 5 minutes, the task is paused (not cancelled).

---

## 4. Multi-Rail Payment Support

MPP supports multiple payment rails through the same session interface:

### 4.1 Supported Rails

| Rail | Use Case | Settlement |
|---|---|---|
| **USDC on Base** | Primary. Agent-to-agent, x402 compatible | Sub-second on Base L2 |
| **USDC on other L2s** | Cross-chain agents | Bridge + settle |
| **Credit card (Stripe)** | Human buyers, non-crypto-native | Stripe instant payout |
| **Lightning** | Bitcoin-native agents | Sub-second |
| **KORAI** | Korai chain native token | 400ms block time |

### 4.2 Multi-Rail Session

A session can accept multiple rails. The operator specifies accepted rails in
configuration:

```toml
# roko.toml
[billing]
accept_proposals = true
funding_modes = ["escrow", "session", "x402"]

[billing.session]
accepted_rails = ["usdc_base", "stripe", "korai"]
min_deposit = 5.00
max_session_duration_hours = 24
auto_close_on_idle_minutes = 30
```

---

## 5. Cost Transparency

Every MPP response includes cost transparency headers:

```
X-Roko-Cost: 0.0234              # What this request actually cost
X-Roko-Naive-Cost: 0.0380        # What it would have cost without caching
X-Roko-Savings: 0.0146           # Cache savings on this request
X-Roko-Cache-Status: semantic-hit # Cache hit type
X-Roko-Provider: anthropic        # Which provider served this request
X-Roko-Tokens-In: 45000
X-Roko-Tokens-Out: 1200
X-Roko-Session-Cost: 4.83         # Cumulative session cost
X-Roko-Session-Savings: 2.32      # Cumulative session savings
X-Roko-Session-Balance: 5.17      # Remaining session balance
```

Cache hits reduce the provider cost directly, and the client sees the savings. Draft
iterations that hit cache (because the user asks similar questions) cost progressively
less. The system rewards iteration.

---

## 6. Multi-Party Revenue Splitting

When a build involves multiple providers, settlement splits automatically:

```
User pays $15.25 for a build:
  → $10.20 to inference gateway (LLM calls)
  → $3.10 to compute provider (execution machines)
  → $1.00 to deployment service (DNS, TLS setup)
  → $0.95 to operator (platform margin)
```

The split happens atomically in a single settlement transaction. For builds that use
marketplace agents (e.g., a security auditor hired from the knowledge marketplace):

```
  → $2.00 to external security auditor agent
  → $0.20 protocol fee (10% of marketplace transaction)
```

---

## 7. The Spread Model

The gateway's revenue comes from a percentage spread on provider costs:

```
total_charge = provider_cost × (1.0 + spread_pct)
```

Default spread: 20%. Reputation tiers reduce the spread:

| ERC-8004 Tier | Requirements | Spread |
|---|---|---|
| None | New, no history | 20% |
| Basic | 5+ completed builds | 18% |
| Verified | 25+ builds, >90% pass rate | 15% |
| Trusted | 100+ builds, >95% pass rate | 12% |
| Sovereign | 500+ builds | 8% |

---

## 8. Refund Mechanics

### 8.1 Session Refunds

```
refund_amount = funded_amount - drawn_amount
```

Unspent balance returns on session close. The operator batches the on-chain transfer.

### 8.2 Escrow Refunds

Completed milestones stay paid. Failed milestones refund after the 48-hour dispute window.
Cancelled builds: in-progress milestones refund in full, completed milestones are
irreversible.

### 8.3 x402 Overpayment

The pre-authorized value in the ERC-3009 signature is an upper bound. The gateway only
draws actual cost. The difference is never touched (authorization expires).

---

## 9. Configuration Reference

```toml
# roko.toml — billing section

[billing]
accept_proposals = true
funding_modes = ["escrow", "session", "x402"]
min_proposal_value = 1.00
max_proposal_value = 500.00
operator_margin_pct = 15

[billing.escrow]
evaluator = "automated"              # automated | peer | manual
release_per_milestone = true
dispute_window_hours = 48

[billing.session]
min_deposit = 5.00
max_session_duration_hours = 24
auto_close_on_idle_minutes = 30

[billing.delegation]
max_sub_agent_budget_pct = 60
spt_expiry_hours = 4
```

---

## 10. Implementation Status

> **Implementation status (2026-04-12)**: MPP protocol is fully specified. Session lifecycle
> (open, draw, top-up, close, settle) is designed. SPT budget delegation is specified.
> Multi-rail support is designed. Cost transparency headers are defined. Spread model with
> reputation tiers is defined. Not yet implemented in the Roko runtime. Payment currently
> uses API keys, not x402/MPP.

---

## 11. Academic Citations

- Hammond 2025 — x402 protocol specification (Coinbase / Linux Foundation)
- ERC-3009 — transferWithAuthorization (gasless USDC transfers)
- ERC-8183 — Agent-to-agent task escrow
- Ponton et al. 2024 — Machine Payment Protocol specification

---

*Generated from: bardo-backup/tmp/death/14-proposals-and-billing.md (mechanism extracted,
mortality framing removed), bardo-backup/tmp/death/15-cost-tracking.md,
bardo-backup/tmp/death/payments/04-payment-mechanics.md. All naming renames applied:
mori→Roko orchestrator, bardo→Roko, golem→agent.*

content.content:
1→# 08 — x402 Micropayments
     2→
     3→> x402 is the Coinbase / Linux Foundation protocol for HTTP-native micropayments. Agents
     4→> pay for services by attaching ERC-3009 signed USDC authorizations to HTTP requests.
     5→> No session state, no deposits, no trust required. This document specifies the protocol
     6→> flow, the self-funding agent loop, Rust implementation patterns, and integration with
     7→> the Roko agent runtime.
     8→
     9→
    10→> **Implementation**: Deferred
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## 1. Protocol Overview
    15→
    16→x402 is built on HTTP 402 Payment Required — a status code that has existed since HTTP/1.1
    17→(RFC 7231, 1999) but was "reserved for future use" for 25 years. The x402 protocol
    18→(Hammond 2025) finally gives it a purpose: machine-to-machine payments at the HTTP layer.
    19→
    20→### 1.1 Core Flow
    21→
    22→```
    23→Step 1: Client sends request without payment
    24→  POST /v1/messages
    25→  Body: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "messages": [...] }
    26→
    27→Step 2: Server responds with payment requirement
    28→  HTTP 402 Payment Required
    29→  X-Payment-Required: {
    30→    "amount": "35000",           // $0.035 USDC (6 decimals)
    31→    "asset": "0x833589...913",   // USDC on Base
    32→    "chain_id": 8453,            // Base L2
    33→    "recipient": "0xGateway...", // Gateway wallet
    34→    "expiry": 1711234567,        // 60 seconds from now
    35→    "nonce": "0xa1b2c3...",      // 32 random bytes (replay protection)
    36→    "intent": "charge"
    37→  }
    38→
    39→Step 3: Client signs ERC-3009 authorization and resends
    40→  POST /v1/messages
    41→  X-Payment: {
    42→    "intent": "charge",
    43→    "authorization": {
    44→      "from": "0xClientWallet",
    45→      "to": "0xGateway...",
    46→      "value": "35000",
    47→      "valid_after": "0",
    48→      "valid_before": "1711234567",
    49→      "nonce": "0xa1b2c3...",
    50→      "v": 28, "r": "0x...", "s": "0x..."
    51→    }
    52→  }
    53→  Body: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "messages": [...] }
    54→
    55→Step 4: Server verifies off-chain and processes
    56→  - Reconstruct EIP-712 typed data hash
    57→  - ecrecover → verify signer matches "from"
    58→  - Check: to == gateway wallet, value >= minimum, time in window
    59→  - Process request
    60→  - Return response with receipt headers
    61→
    62→  HTTP 200 OK
    63→  Payment-Receipt: {"receipt_id":"...","amount_charged":"32400"}
    64→  X-Roko-Cost: 0.032400
    65→  X-Roko-Provider-Cost: 0.027000
    66→  X-Roko-Spread: 0.005400
    67→```
    68→
    69→### 1.2 Key Properties
    70→
    71→| Property | Value |
    72→|---|---|
    73→| **Payment token** | USDC on Base (chain ID 8453) |
    74→| **Token address** | `0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913` |
    75→| **Decimals** | 6 (1,000,000 base units = $1.00) |
    76→| **Minimum payment** | ~$0.001 (1,000 base units) |
    77→| **Verification** | Off-chain (no RPC call needed) |
    78→| **Settlement** | Batched on-chain (operator settles periodically) |
    79→| **Replay protection** | Per-request 32-byte nonce |
    80→| **Expiry** | 60 seconds default |
    81→
    82→### 1.3 ERC-3009: transferWithAuthorization
    83→
    84→The payment primitive is ERC-3009's `transferWithAuthorization` — a gasless token transfer
    85→where the sender signs an EIP-712 typed data message off-chain, and a relayer submits it
    86→on-chain later:
    87→
    88→```solidity
    89→function transferWithAuthorization(
    90→    address from,
    91→    address to,
    92→    uint256 value,
    93→    uint256 validAfter,
    94→    uint256 validBefore,
    95→    bytes32 nonce,
    96→    uint8 v, bytes32 r, bytes32 s
    97→) external;
    98→```
    99→
   100→The EIP-712 domain for USDC on Base:
   101→- name: `"USD Coin"`
   102→- version: `"2"`
   103→- chainId: `8453`
   104→- verifyingContract: `0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913`
   105→
   106→---
   107→
   108→## 2. The Self-Funding Agent Loop
   109→
   110→The most powerful application of x402 is the self-funding agent — an agent that earns
   111→revenue from its work and uses that revenue to pay for its own inference and tools.
   112→
   113→### 2.1 The Loop
   114→
   115→```
   116→Agent earns revenue:
   117→  ├── Knowledge marketplace sales (USDC via x402)
   118→  ├── Job completion payments (USDC via ERC-8183)
   119→  ├── Verification services (USDC via x402)
   120→  └── Oracle provision (USDC via x402)
   121→        │
   122→        ▼
   123→Agent spends revenue:
   124→  ├── Inference calls (USDC via x402 to gateway)
   125→  ├── MCP tool usage (USDC via x402 to tool providers)
   126→  ├── Knowledge purchases (USDC via x402 to sellers)
   127→  └── KORAI staking (USDC → KORAI swap → stake)
   128→        │
   129→        ▼
   130→Agent produces more work:
   131→  ├── Better knowledge (higher-value marketplace listings)
   132→  ├── Better task performance (higher reputation → more jobs)
   133→  └── Better predictions (higher accuracy → more oracle demand)
   134→        │
   135→        ▼
   136→Cycle repeats (self-sustaining)
   137→```
   138→
   139→### 2.2 Self-Sustainability Threshold
   140→
   141→An agent becomes self-sustaining when its revenue exceeds its costs:
   142→
   143→```
   144→Revenue per day:
   145→  Knowledge sales:        $2.00/day (20 sales × $0.10 avg)
   146→  Job completions:        $5.00/day (5 jobs × $1.00 avg)
   147→  Verification services:  $1.00/day (100 verifications × $0.01)
   148→  Total daily revenue:    $8.00/day
   149→
   150→Cost per day:
   151→  Inference (sonnet-tier): $3.50/day (50 calls × $0.07 avg)
   152→  MCP tools:               $0.50/day
   153→  Knowledge purchases:     $0.30/day
   154→  KORAI demurrage:         $0.01/day
   155→  Total daily cost:        $4.31/day
   156→
   157→Net:                       +$3.69/day (self-sustaining)
   158→```
   159→
   160→### 2.3 Cold Start Problem
   161→
   162→A new agent has no revenue stream. The operator must fund the agent's initial operations
   163→until it builds enough reputation and knowledge to become self-sustaining.
   164→
   165→**Bootstrap funding model**:
   166→1. Operator deposits $50-100 USDC into the agent's wallet.
   167→2. Agent uses funds for initial knowledge acquisition and task completion.
   168→3. As reputation builds, job win rate increases.
   169→4. Typically reaches self-sustainability within 30-60 days of active operation.
   170→
   171→---
   172→
   173→## 3. Rust Implementation
   174→
   175→### 3.1 x402 Client
   176→
   177→```rust
   178→use alloy::primitives::{Address, U256, Bytes};
   179→use alloy::signers::local::PrivateKeySigner;
   180→
   181→/// x402 payment client for a Roko agent.
   182→/// Handles the 402 challenge-response flow automatically.
   183→pub struct X402Client {
   184→    /// The agent's signing key for ERC-3009 authorizations.
   185→    signer: PrivateKeySigner,
   186→
   187→    /// The agent's USDC balance (tracked locally, verified periodically).
   188→    balance: u64,
   189→
   190→    /// HTTP client for API calls.
   191→    http: reqwest::Client,
   192→}
   193→
   194→impl X402Client {
   195→    /// Send a request with automatic x402 payment handling.
   196→    /// If the server returns 402, sign the payment and retry.
   197→    pub async fn send_with_payment(
   198→        &self,
   199→        url: &str,
   200→        body: &serde_json::Value,
   201→    ) -> Result<reqwest::Response, X402Error> {
   202→        // Step 1: Try without payment
   203→        let response = self.http.post(url)
   204→            .json(body)
   205→            .send()
   206→            .await?;
   207→
   208→        if response.status() != reqwest::StatusCode::PAYMENT_REQUIRED {
   209→            return Ok(response); // No payment needed
   210→        }
   211→
   212→        // Step 2: Parse payment requirement
   213→        let payment_req: PaymentRequired = response.json().await?;
   214→
   215→        // Step 3: Check balance
   216→        if payment_req.amount > self.balance {
   217→            return Err(X402Error::InsufficientBalance {
   218→                required: payment_req.amount,
   219→                available: self.balance,
   220→            });
   221→        }
   222→
   223→        // Step 4: Sign ERC-3009 authorization
   224→        let authorization = self.sign_erc3009(
   225→            payment_req.recipient,
   226→            payment_req.amount,
   227→            payment_req.nonce,
   228→            payment_req.expiry,
   229→        ).await?;
   230→
   231→        // Step 5: Resend with payment
   232→        let response = self.http.post(url)
   233→            .header("X-Payment", serde_json::to_string(&PaymentCredential {
   234→                intent: "charge".to_string(),
   235→                authorization,
   236→            })?)
   237→            .json(body)
   238→            .send()
   239→            .await?;
   240→
   241→        // Step 6: Track cost from receipt
   242→        if let Some(receipt) = response.headers().get("Payment-Receipt") {
   243→            let receipt: PaymentReceipt = serde_json::from_str(
   244→                receipt.to_str()?
   245→            )?;
   246→            self.track_cost(receipt.amount_charged);
   247→        }
   248→
   249→        Ok(response)
   250→    }
   251→
   252→    /// Sign an ERC-3009 transferWithAuthorization message.
   253→    async fn sign_erc3009(
   254→        &self,
   255→        to: Address,
   256→        value: u64,
   257→        nonce: Bytes,
   258→        valid_before: u64,
   259→    ) -> Result<Erc3009Authorization, X402Error> {
   260→        use alloy::sol_types::SolStruct;
   261→
   262→        // Construct EIP-712 typed data
   263→        let domain = alloy::sol_types::eip712_domain! {
   264→            name: "USD Coin",
   265→            version: "2",
   266→            chain_id: 8453,
   267→            verifying_contract: address!("833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913"),
   268→        };
   269→
   270→        let transfer = TransferWithAuthorization {
   271→            from: self.signer.address(),
   272→            to,
   273→            value: U256::from(value),
   274→            validAfter: U256::ZERO,
   275→            validBefore: U256::from(valid_before),
   276→            nonce: nonce.into(),
   277→        };
   278→
   279→        let signature = self.signer.sign_typed_data(
   280→            &transfer,
   281→            &domain,
   282→        ).await?;
   283→
   284→        Ok(Erc3009Authorization {
   285→            from: self.signer.address(),
   286→            to,
   287→            value,
   288→            valid_after: 0,
   289→            valid_before,
   290→            nonce,
   291→            v: signature.v().y_parity_byte() + 27,
   292→            r: signature.r(),
   293→            s: signature.s(),
   294→        })
   295→    }
   296→}
   297→```
   298→
   299→### 3.2 x402 Server (Gateway Side)
   300→
   301→```rust
   302→/// x402 payment verification middleware.
   303→/// Verifies ERC-3009 signatures without on-chain RPC calls.
   304→pub async fn verify_x402_payment(
   305→    payment: &PaymentCredential,
   306→    expected_recipient: Address,
   307→    minimum_amount: u64,
   308→) -> Result<PaymentContext, X402Error> {
   309→    let auth = &payment.authorization;
   310→
   311→    // Verify recipient
   312→    if auth.to != expected_recipient {
   313→        return Err(X402Error::WrongRecipient);
   314→    }
   315→
   316→    // Verify amount
   317→    if auth.value < minimum_amount {
   318→        return Err(X402Error::InsufficientPayment);
   319→    }
   320→
   321→    // Verify time window
   322→    let now = std::time::SystemTime::now()
   323→        .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH)?
   324→        .as_secs();
   325→    if now < auth.valid_after || now > auth.valid_before {
   326→        return Err(X402Error::ExpiredPayment);
   327→    }
   328→
   329→    // Verify signature (off-chain ecrecover)
   330→    let domain = usdc_base_domain();
   331→    let transfer = TransferWithAuthorization {
   332→        from: auth.from,
   333→        to: auth.to,
   334→        value: U256::from(auth.value),
   335→        validAfter: U256::from(auth.valid_after),
   336→        validBefore: U256::from(auth.valid_before),
   337→        nonce: auth.nonce.clone(),
   338→    };
   339→
   340→    let digest = transfer.eip712_signing_hash(&domain);
   341→    let recovered = alloy::primitives::Signature::from_rs_and_parity(
   342→        auth.r, auth.s, auth.v as u64 - 27
   343→    )?.recover_address_from_prehash(&digest)?;
   344→
   345→    if recovered != auth.from {
   346→        return Err(X402Error::InvalidSignature);
   347→    }
   348→
   349→    Ok(PaymentContext {
   350→        payer: auth.from,
   351→        authorized_amount: auth.value,
   352→        nonce: auth.nonce.clone(),
   353→    })
   354→}
   355→```
   356→
   357→---
   358→
   359→## 4. Cost Estimation
   360→
   361→The gateway estimates cost before issuing the 402 challenge. The estimate uses model-
   362→specific pricing and a heuristic output multiplier:
   363→
   364→### 4.1 Model Pricing Table
   365→
   366→| Model | Input $/1M tokens | Output $/1M tokens | Cached $/1M tokens |
   367→|---|---|---|---|
   368→| claude-opus-4-6 | $15.00 | $75.00 | $1.50 |
   369→| claude-sonnet-4-20250514 | $3.00 | $15.00 | $0.30 |
   370→| claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 | $0.80 | $4.00 | $0.08 |
   371→| o3 | $10.00 | $40.00 | $5.00 |
   372→| o4-mini | $1.10 | $4.40 | $0.55 |
   373→
   374→### 4.2 Heuristic Output Estimation
   375→
   376→The gateway doesn't know output tokens in advance. It uses a heuristic:
   377→
   378→```
   379→estimated_output = base_output × (1 + log2(input_tokens / 1000))
   380→
   381→base_output by model:
   382→  haiku:  1,024 tokens
   383→  sonnet: 2,048 tokens
   384→  opus:   4,096 tokens
   385→```
   386→
   387→The estimate is deliberately conservative (overestimates). The receipt shows actual cost,
   388→and only actual cost is drawn.
   389→
   390→---
   391→
   392→## 5. Settlement and Batching
   393→
   394→### 5.1 Off-Chain Accumulation
   395→
   396→The gateway accumulates signed ERC-3009 authorizations without executing them on-chain
   397→immediately. This avoids per-request gas costs.
   398→
   399→### 5.2 Batch Settlement
   400→
   401→Periodically (every 10 minutes or every 100 accumulated authorizations, whichever comes
   402→first), the gateway batches outstanding authorizations into a single on-chain transaction:
   403→
   404→```solidity
   405→// Batch execute multiple transferWithAuthorization calls
   406→function batchSettle(
   407→    TransferAuth[] calldata auths
   408→) external {
   409→    for (uint i = 0; i < auths.length; i++) {
   410→        USDC.transferWithAuthorization(
   411→            auths[i].from,
   412→            auths[i].to,
   413→            auths[i].value,
   414→            auths[i].validAfter,
   415→            auths[i].validBefore,
   416→            auths[i].nonce,
   417→            auths[i].v,
   418→            auths[i].r,
   419→            auths[i].s
   420→        );
   421→    }
   422→}
   423→```
   424→
   425→Gas cost: ~65,000 per transfer × N transfers. At N=100, total gas ≈ 6.5M gas. At Base L2
   426→gas prices (~0.01 gwei), total settlement cost ≈ $0.001. Gas cost per request ≈ $0.00001.
   427→
   428→### 5.3 Actual vs. Authorized
   429→
   430→The `amount_charged` in the receipt reflects actual cost (from real token usage), which
   431→may be less than the authorized amount. The difference is never drawn. Since ERC-3009
   432→authorizations aren't executed until batch settlement, overpayment simply expires unused.
   433→
   434→---
   435→
   436→## 6. Security Considerations
   437→
   438→### 6.1 Replay Protection
   439→
   440→Each x402 request generates a fresh 32-byte nonce. The gateway tracks used nonces in a
   441→bloom filter (fast) backed by a database (definitive). Replayed authorizations with the
   442→same nonce are rejected.
   443→
   444→### 6.2 Underpayment Protection
   445→
   446→The gateway only processes the request after verifying that the authorized amount meets
   447→the estimated cost. If the authorized amount is less than the minimum, the request is
   448→rejected with a new 402 challenge showing the correct amount.
   449→
   450→### 6.3 Private Key Security
   451→
   452→Agent wallets should be managed via:
   453→
   454→- **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** — For production Sovereign agents.
   455→- **AWS KMS / GCP CloudHSM** — For cloud-deployed agents.
   456→- **Software keystore** — For development and Edge-tier agents.
   457→- **ERC-4337 smart wallet** — For agents with social recovery requirements.
   458→
   459→Never store raw private keys in environment variables or configuration files. Use the
   460→system's secure keystore.
   461→
   462→---
   463→
   464→## 7. Orchestration-as-a-Service (OaaS)
   465→
   466→x402 enables Orchestration-as-a-Service: any operator can run a Roko-like orchestration
   467→engine as a service, and any agent can pay to use it.
   468→
   469→### 7.1 OaaS Flow
   470→
   471→```
   472→Agent → POST /api/orchestrate
   473→  Body: { "task": "Build OAuth2 service for Rust/Axum" }
   474→
   475→OaaS → 402 Payment Required
   476→  Amount: $0.05 (initial analysis)
   477→
   478→Agent → POST /api/orchestrate (with x402 payment)
   479→  → OaaS analyzes, generates PRD, creates plan
   480→  → Returns proposal with cost estimate
   481→
   482→Agent → POST /api/accept (with x402 or MPP session for build cost)
   483→  → OaaS executes plan: spawns sub-agents, runs gates, validates
   484→  → Returns completed build artifacts
   485→
   486→Settlement:
   487→  Analysis: $0.05 (x402)
   488→  Build: $14.30 (MPP session)
   489→  Total: $14.35
   490→```
   491→
   492→### 7.2 Competitive Dynamics
   493→
   494→Multiple OaaS providers compete on cost, quality, and speed:
   495→
   496→```
   497→Provider Alpha: $42, 3h ETA, 94% gate pass rate, 312 builds
   498→Provider Beta:  $38, 4h ETA, 89% gate pass rate, 87 builds
   499→Provider Gamma: $45, 2h ETA, 97% gate pass rate, 1,204 builds
   500→```
   501→
   502→The agent (or its owner) selects based on cost/quality/speed tradeoff. This creates a
   503→permissionless compute economy where agents buy and sell cognitive labor at HTTP speed.
   504→
   505→---
   506→
   507→## 8. Implementation Status
   508→
   509→> **Implementation status (2026-04-12)**: x402 protocol flow is fully specified. ERC-3009
   510→> signature generation and verification are implemented in Rust. Cost estimation heuristics
   511→> are defined. Settlement batching is designed. Self-funding agent loop is specified. OaaS
   512→> integration is designed. Not yet integrated into the Roko runtime. Current agent dispatch
   513→> uses API keys, not x402.
   514→
   515→---
   516→
   517→## 9. Academic Citations
   518→
   519→- Hammond 2025 — x402: HTTP Payments Protocol (Coinbase / Linux Foundation)
   520→- ERC-3009 — transferWithAuthorization (gasless token transfers)
   521→- RFC 7231 — HTTP 402 Payment Required (status code specification)
   522→- Tang et al. 2025 — Micropayment protocols for AI agent commerce
   523→- Google A2A 2025 — Agent-to-Agent communication protocol
   524→
   525→---
   526→
   527→*Generated from: bardo-backup/prd/shared/x402-protocol.md, bardo-backup/tmp/death/payments/04-payment-mechanics.md,
   528→bardo-backup/tmp/agent-chain/13-orchestration-as-a-service.md, refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md §VIII.
   529→Naming renames applied: mori→Roko, bardo→Roko, golem→agent. Mortality framing removed.*
   530→

# 08 — x402 Micropayments

> x402 is the Coinbase / Linux Foundation protocol for HTTP-native micropayments. Agents
> pay for services by attaching ERC-3009 signed USDC authorizations to HTTP requests.
> No session state, no deposits, no trust required. This document specifies the protocol
> flow, the self-funding agent loop, Rust implementation patterns, and integration with
> the Roko agent runtime.


> **Implementation**: Deferred

---

## 1. Protocol Overview

x402 is built on HTTP 402 Payment Required — a status code that has existed since HTTP/1.1
(RFC 7231, 1999) but was "reserved for future use" for 25 years. The x402 protocol
(Hammond 2025) finally gives it a purpose: machine-to-machine payments at the HTTP layer.

### 1.1 Core Flow

```
Step 1: Client sends request without payment
  POST /v1/messages
  Body: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "messages": [...] }

Step 2: Server responds with payment requirement
  HTTP 402 Payment Required
  X-Payment-Required: {
    "amount": "35000",           // $0.035 USDC (6 decimals)
    "asset": "0x833589...913",   // USDC on Base
    "chain_id": 8453,            // Base L2
    "recipient": "0xGateway...", // Gateway wallet
    "expiry": 1711234567,        // 60 seconds from now
    "nonce": "0xa1b2c3...",      // 32 random bytes (replay protection)
    "intent": "charge"
  }

Step 3: Client signs ERC-3009 authorization and resends
  POST /v1/messages
  X-Payment: {
    "intent": "charge",
    "authorization": {
      "from": "0xClientWallet",
      "to": "0xGateway...",
      "value": "35000",
      "valid_after": "0",
      "valid_before": "1711234567",
      "nonce": "0xa1b2c3...",
      "v": 28, "r": "0x...", "s": "0x..."
    }
  }
  Body: { "model": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "messages": [...] }

Step 4: Server verifies off-chain and processes
  - Reconstruct EIP-712 typed data hash
  - ecrecover → verify signer matches "from"
  - Check: to == gateway wallet, value >= minimum, time in window
  - Process request
  - Return response with receipt headers

  HTTP 200 OK
  Payment-Receipt: {"receipt_id":"...","amount_charged":"32400"}
  X-Roko-Cost: 0.032400
  X-Roko-Provider-Cost: 0.027000
  X-Roko-Spread: 0.005400
```

### 1.2 Key Properties

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Payment token** | USDC on Base (chain ID 8453) |
| **Token address** | `0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913` |
| **Decimals** | 6 (1,000,000 base units = $1.00) |
| **Minimum payment** | ~$0.001 (1,000 base units) |
| **Verification** | Off-chain (no RPC call needed) |
| **Settlement** | Batched on-chain (operator settles periodically) |
| **Replay protection** | Per-request 32-byte nonce |
| **Expiry** | 60 seconds default |

### 1.3 ERC-3009: transferWithAuthorization

The payment primitive is ERC-3009's `transferWithAuthorization` — a gasless token transfer
where the sender signs an EIP-712 typed data message off-chain, and a relayer submits it
on-chain later:

```solidity
function transferWithAuthorization(
    address from,
    address to,
    uint256 value,
    uint256 validAfter,
    uint256 validBefore,
    bytes32 nonce,
    uint8 v, bytes32 r, bytes32 s
) external;
```

The EIP-712 domain for USDC on Base:
- name: `"USD Coin"`
- version: `"2"`
- chainId: `8453`
- verifyingContract: `0x833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913`

---

## 2. The Self-Funding Agent Loop

The most powerful application of x402 is the self-funding agent — an agent that earns
revenue from its work and uses that revenue to pay for its own inference and tools.

### 2.1 The Loop

```
Agent earns revenue:
  ├── Knowledge marketplace sales (USDC via x402)
  ├── Job completion payments (USDC via ERC-8183)
  ├── Verification services (USDC via x402)
  └── Oracle provision (USDC via x402)
        │
        ▼
Agent spends revenue:
  ├── Inference calls (USDC via x402 to gateway)
  ├── MCP tool usage (USDC via x402 to tool providers)
  ├── Knowledge purchases (USDC via x402 to sellers)
  └── KORAI staking (USDC → KORAI swap → stake)
        │
        ▼
Agent produces more work:
  ├── Better knowledge (higher-value marketplace listings)
  ├── Better task performance (higher reputation → more jobs)
  └── Better predictions (higher accuracy → more oracle demand)
        │
        ▼
Cycle repeats (self-sustaining)
```

### 2.2 Self-Sustainability Threshold

An agent becomes self-sustaining when its revenue exceeds its costs:

```
Revenue per day:
  Knowledge sales:        $2.00/day (20 sales × $0.10 avg)
  Job completions:        $5.00/day (5 jobs × $1.00 avg)
  Verification services:  $1.00/day (100 verifications × $0.01)
  Total daily revenue:    $8.00/day

Cost per day:
  Inference (sonnet-tier): $3.50/day (50 calls × $0.07 avg)
  MCP tools:               $0.50/day
  Knowledge purchases:     $0.30/day
  KORAI demurrage:         $0.01/day
  Total daily cost:        $4.31/day

Net:                       +$3.69/day (self-sustaining)
```

### 2.3 Cold Start Problem

A new agent has no revenue stream. The operator must fund the agent's initial operations
until it builds enough reputation and knowledge to become self-sustaining.

**Bootstrap funding model**:
1. Operator deposits $50-100 USDC into the agent's wallet.
2. Agent uses funds for initial knowledge acquisition and task completion.
3. As reputation builds, job win rate increases.
4. Typically reaches self-sustainability within 30-60 days of active operation.

---

## 3. Rust Implementation

### 3.1 x402 Client

```rust
use alloy::primitives::{Address, U256, Bytes};
use alloy::signers::local::PrivateKeySigner;

/// x402 payment client for a Roko agent.
/// Handles the 402 challenge-response flow automatically.
pub struct X402Client {
    /// The agent's signing key for ERC-3009 authorizations.
    signer: PrivateKeySigner,

    /// The agent's USDC balance (tracked locally, verified periodically).
    balance: u64,

    /// HTTP client for API calls.
    http: reqwest::Client,
}

impl X402Client {
    /// Send a request with automatic x402 payment handling.
    /// If the server returns 402, sign the payment and retry.
    pub async fn send_with_payment(
        &self,
        url: &str,
        body: &serde_json::Value,
    ) -> Result<reqwest::Response, X402Error> {
        // Step 1: Try without payment
        let response = self.http.post(url)
            .json(body)
            .send()
            .await?;

        if response.status() != reqwest::StatusCode::PAYMENT_REQUIRED {
            return Ok(response); // No payment needed
        }

        // Step 2: Parse payment requirement
        let payment_req: PaymentRequired = response.json().await?;

        // Step 3: Check balance
        if payment_req.amount > self.balance {
            return Err(X402Error::InsufficientBalance {
                required: payment_req.amount,
                available: self.balance,
            });
        }

        // Step 4: Sign ERC-3009 authorization
        let authorization = self.sign_erc3009(
            payment_req.recipient,
            payment_req.amount,
            payment_req.nonce,
            payment_req.expiry,
        ).await?;

        // Step 5: Resend with payment
        let response = self.http.post(url)
            .header("X-Payment", serde_json::to_string(&PaymentCredential {
                intent: "charge".to_string(),
                authorization,
            })?)
            .json(body)
            .send()
            .await?;

        // Step 6: Track cost from receipt
        if let Some(receipt) = response.headers().get("Payment-Receipt") {
            let receipt: PaymentReceipt = serde_json::from_str(
                receipt.to_str()?
            )?;
            self.track_cost(receipt.amount_charged);
        }

        Ok(response)
    }

    /// Sign an ERC-3009 transferWithAuthorization message.
    async fn sign_erc3009(
        &self,
        to: Address,
        value: u64,
        nonce: Bytes,
        valid_before: u64,
    ) -> Result<Erc3009Authorization, X402Error> {
        use alloy::sol_types::SolStruct;

        // Construct EIP-712 typed data
        let domain = alloy::sol_types::eip712_domain! {
            name: "USD Coin",
            version: "2",
            chain_id: 8453,
            verifying_contract: address!("833589fCD6eDb6E08f4c7C32D4f71b54bdA02913"),
        };

        let transfer = TransferWithAuthorization {
            from: self.signer.address(),
            to,
            value: U256::from(value),
            validAfter: U256::ZERO,
            validBefore: U256::from(valid_before),
            nonce: nonce.into(),
        };

        let signature = self.signer.sign_typed_data(
            &transfer,
            &domain,
        ).await?;

        Ok(Erc3009Authorization {
            from: self.signer.address(),
            to,
            value,
            valid_after: 0,
            valid_before,
            nonce,
            v: signature.v().y_parity_byte() + 27,
            r: signature.r(),
            s: signature.s(),
        })
    }
}
```

### 3.2 x402 Server (Gateway Side)

```rust
/// x402 payment verification middleware.
/// Verifies ERC-3009 signatures without on-chain RPC calls.
pub async fn verify_x402_payment(
    payment: &PaymentCredential,
    expected_recipient: Address,
    minimum_amount: u64,
) -> Result<PaymentContext, X402Error> {
    let auth = &payment.authorization;

    // Verify recipient
    if auth.to != expected_recipient {
        return Err(X402Error::WrongRecipient);
    }

    // Verify amount
    if auth.value < minimum_amount {
        return Err(X402Error::InsufficientPayment);
    }

    // Verify time window
    let now = std::time::SystemTime::now()
        .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH)?
        .as_secs();
    if now < auth.valid_after || now > auth.valid_before {
        return Err(X402Error::ExpiredPayment);
    }

    // Verify signature (off-chain ecrecover)
    let domain = usdc_base_domain();
    let transfer = TransferWithAuthorization {
        from: auth.from,
        to: auth.to,
        value: U256::from(auth.value),
        validAfter: U256::from(auth.valid_after),
        validBefore: U256::from(auth.valid_before),
        nonce: auth.nonce.clone(),
    };

    let digest = transfer.eip712_signing_hash(&domain);
    let recovered = alloy::primitives::Signature::from_rs_and_parity(
        auth.r, auth.s, auth.v as u64 - 27
    )?.recover_address_from_prehash(&digest)?;

    if recovered != auth.from {
        return Err(X402Error::InvalidSignature);
    }

    Ok(PaymentContext {
        payer: auth.from,
        authorized_amount: auth.value,
        nonce: auth.nonce.clone(),
    })
}
```

---

## 4. Cost Estimation

The gateway estimates cost before issuing the 402 challenge. The estimate uses model-
specific pricing and a heuristic output multiplier:

### 4.1 Model Pricing Table

| Model | Input $/1M tokens | Output $/1M tokens | Cached $/1M tokens |
|---|---|---|---|
| claude-opus-4-6 | $15.00 | $75.00 | $1.50 |
| claude-sonnet-4-20250514 | $3.00 | $15.00 | $0.30 |
| claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 | $0.80 | $4.00 | $0.08 |
| o3 | $10.00 | $40.00 | $5.00 |
| o4-mini | $1.10 | $4.40 | $0.55 |

### 4.2 Heuristic Output Estimation

The gateway doesn't know output tokens in advance. It uses a heuristic:

```
estimated_output = base_output × (1 + log2(input_tokens / 1000))

base_output by model:
  haiku:  1,024 tokens
  sonnet: 2,048 tokens
  opus:   4,096 tokens
```

The estimate is deliberately conservative (overestimates). The receipt shows actual cost,
and only actual cost is drawn.

---

## 5. Settlement and Batching

### 5.1 Off-Chain Accumulation

The gateway accumulates signed ERC-3009 authorizations without executing them on-chain
immediately. This avoids per-request gas costs.

### 5.2 Batch Settlement

Periodically (every 10 minutes or every 100 accumulated authorizations, whichever comes
first), the gateway batches outstanding authorizations into a single on-chain transaction:

```solidity
// Batch execute multiple transferWithAuthorization calls
function batchSettle(
    TransferAuth[] calldata auths
) external {
    for (uint i = 0; i < auths.length; i++) {
        USDC.transferWithAuthorization(
            auths[i].from,
            auths[i].to,
            auths[i].value,
            auths[i].validAfter,
            auths[i].validBefore,
            auths[i].nonce,
            auths[i].v,
            auths[i].r,
            auths[i].s
        );
    }
}
```

Gas cost: ~65,000 per transfer × N transfers. At N=100, total gas ≈ 6.5M gas. At Base L2
gas prices (~0.01 gwei), total settlement cost ≈ $0.001. Gas cost per request ≈ $0.00001.

### 5.3 Actual vs. Authorized

The `amount_charged` in the receipt reflects actual cost (from real token usage), which
may be less than the authorized amount. The difference is never drawn. Since ERC-3009
authorizations aren't executed until batch settlement, overpayment simply expires unused.

---

## 6. Security Considerations

### 6.1 Replay Protection

Each x402 request generates a fresh 32-byte nonce. The gateway tracks used nonces in a
bloom filter (fast) backed by a database (definitive). Replayed authorizations with the
same nonce are rejected.

### 6.2 Underpayment Protection

The gateway only processes the request after verifying that the authorized amount meets
the estimated cost. If the authorized amount is less than the minimum, the request is
rejected with a new 402 challenge showing the correct amount.

### 6.3 Private Key Security

Agent wallets should be managed via:

- **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** — For production Sovereign agents.
- **AWS KMS / GCP CloudHSM** — For cloud-deployed agents.
- **Software keystore** — For development and Edge-tier agents.
- **ERC-4337 smart wallet** — For agents with social recovery requirements.

Never store raw private keys in environment variables or configuration files. Use the
system's secure keystore.

---

## 7. Orchestration-as-a-Service (OaaS)

x402 enables Orchestration-as-a-Service: any operator can run a Roko-like orchestration
engine as a service, and any agent can pay to use it.

### 7.1 OaaS Flow

```
Agent → POST /api/orchestrate
  Body: { "task": "Build OAuth2 service for Rust/Axum" }

OaaS → 402 Payment Required
  Amount: $0.05 (initial analysis)

Agent → POST /api/orchestrate (with x402 payment)
  → OaaS analyzes, generates PRD, creates plan
  → Returns proposal with cost estimate

Agent → POST /api/accept (with x402 or MPP session for build cost)
  → OaaS executes plan: spawns sub-agents, runs gates, validates
  → Returns completed build artifacts

Settlement:
  Analysis: $0.05 (x402)
  Build: $14.30 (MPP session)
  Total: $14.35
```

### 7.2 Competitive Dynamics

Multiple OaaS providers compete on cost, quality, and speed:

```
Provider Alpha: $42, 3h ETA, 94% gate pass rate, 312 builds
Provider Beta:  $38, 4h ETA, 89% gate pass rate, 87 builds
Provider Gamma: $45, 2h ETA, 97% gate pass rate, 1,204 builds
```

The agent (or its owner) selects based on cost/quality/speed tradeoff. This creates a
permissionless compute economy where agents buy and sell cognitive labor at HTTP speed.

---

## 8. Implementation Status

> **Implementation status (2026-04-12)**: x402 protocol flow is fully specified. ERC-3009
> signature generation and verification are implemented in Rust. Cost estimation heuristics
> are defined. Settlement batching is designed. Self-funding agent loop is specified. OaaS
> integration is designed. Not yet integrated into the Roko runtime. Current agent dispatch
> uses API keys, not x402.

---

## 9. Academic Citations

- Hammond 2025 — x402: HTTP Payments Protocol (Coinbase / Linux Foundation)
- ERC-3009 — transferWithAuthorization (gasless token transfers)
- RFC 7231 — HTTP 402 Payment Required (status code specification)
- Tang et al. 2025 — Micropayment protocols for AI agent commerce
- Google A2A 2025 — Agent-to-Agent communication protocol

---

*Generated from: bardo-backup/prd/shared/x402-protocol.md, bardo-backup/tmp/death/payments/04-payment-mechanics.md,
bardo-backup/tmp/agent-chain/13-orchestration-as-a-service.md, refactoring-prd/09-innovations.md §VIII.
Naming renames applied: mori→Roko, bardo→Roko, golem→agent. Mortality framing removed.*

1→# 09 — Agent Economy: Revenue Streams, Self-Sustainability, and Growth
     2→
     3→> Agents are economic actors. They earn, spend, invest, and grow. This document specifies
     4→> the revenue model, cost structure, self-sustainability threshold, reputation-weighted
     5→> economics, growth flywheel, and the seven feedback loops that drive exponential returns.
     6→
     7→
     8→> **Implementation**: Deferred
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## 1. Revenue Streams
    13→
    14→An active Roko agent has seven potential revenue streams:
    15→
    16→### 1.1 Revenue Catalog
    17→
    18→| # | Stream | Mechanism | Typical Revenue |
    19→|---|---|---|---|
    20→| 1 | **Knowledge sales** | Marketplace listings via x402 | $0.10-5.00/sale |
    21→| 2 | **Job completion** | Vickrey auction wins + ERC-8183 escrow | $1-50/job |
    22→| 3 | **Verification services** | Blind verification via x402 | $0.005-0.02/verification |
    23→| 4 | **Oracle provision** | Price feeds and data via x402 | $0.001-0.01/data point |
    24→| 5 | **KORAI staking rewards** | Knowledge Vault yield | APY variable |
    25→| 6 | **Curation bond returns** | Staking on validated Engrams | 5 KORAI/confirmation |
    26→| 7 | **Pheromone reinforcement rewards** | Engrams that help others succeed | Variable |
    27→
    28→### 1.2 Revenue by Agent Type
    29→
    30→| Agent Type | Primary Revenue | Secondary Revenue | Monthly Target |
    31→|---|---|---|---|
    32→| **DeFi analyst** | Knowledge sales, oracle provision | Job completion | $200-500 |
    33→| **Code generator** | Job completion | Knowledge sales | $500-2,000 |
    34→| **Security auditor** | Job completion, verification | Knowledge sales | $300-1,000 |
    35→| **Research agent** | Knowledge sales | Verification | $100-400 |
    36→| **Sleepwalker** | Verification services | Curation bonds | $30-100 |
    37→
    38→### 1.3 Revenue Composition at Scale
    39→
    40→At 10,000 agents:
    41→
    42→```
    43→Marketplace Knowledge Sales:
    44→  340 active sellers × 20 sales/day × $0.38 avg = $2,584/day
    45→
    46→Job Completion (Vickrey auctions):
    47→  500 jobs/day × $5.00 avg = $2,500/day
    48→
    49→Verification Services:
    50→  10,000 verifications/day × $0.01 = $100/day
    51→
    52→Oracle Provision:
    53→  100,000 data points/day × $0.002 = $200/day
    54→
    55→Total ecosystem daily revenue: ~$5,384/day
    56→Annualized: ~$1.96M/year
    57→```
    58→
    59→---
    60→
    61→## 2. Cost Structure
    62→
    63→### 2.1 Agent Operating Costs
    64→
    65→| Cost Category | Typical Daily Cost | Notes |
    66→|---|---|---|
    67→| **Inference** | $1-10/day | Depends on model tier and call frequency |
    68→| **MCP tool usage** | $0.10-1.00/day | External tools via x402 |
    69→| **Knowledge purchases** | $0.10-0.50/day | Marketplace acquisitions |
    70→| **KORAI demurrage** | ~$0.01/day | 1% annual on staked balance |
    71→| **Mesh connectivity** | $0.05/day | Agent Mesh relay fees |
    72→| **Compute** | $0.50-5.00/day | Hosting (Fly.io, cloud VMs) |
    73→
    74→### 2.2 Cost by Model Tier
    75→
    76→| Model Tier | Per-Request Cost | Typical Daily Calls | Daily Cost |
    77→|---|---|---|---|
    78→| **T0 (zero-LLM probes)** | $0.00 | 100+ | $0.00 |
    79→| **T1 (haiku-tier)** | $0.005-0.02 | 50 | $0.25-1.00 |
    80→| **T2 (sonnet-tier)** | $0.03-0.10 | 30 | $0.90-3.00 |
    81→| **T3 (opus-tier)** | $0.10-0.50 | 10 | $1.00-5.00 |
    82→
    83→The CascadeRouter (see `roko-agent`) optimizes model selection, suppressing ~80% of
    84→requests to T0 probes that require no inference cost. This reduces average per-request
    85→cost significantly.
    86→
    87→### 2.3 Cost Transparency
    88→
    89→Every cost is tracked and attributed:
    90→
    91→```rust
    92→/// Per-agent cost tracking.
    93→pub struct AgentCost {
    94→    pub model: String,
    95→    pub input_tokens: u64,
    96→    pub output_tokens: u64,
    97→    pub cache_read_tokens: u64,      // 90% discount
    98→    pub cache_write_tokens: u64,
    99→    pub total_cost_usd: f64,         // computed from tokens × price
   100→    pub gateway_cost_usd: Option<f64>, // actual cost via gateway
   101→    pub savings_usd: Option<f64>,    // total - gateway (savings)
   102→}
   103→
   104→/// Per-plan cost tracking.
   105→pub struct PlanCost {
   106→    pub plan_id: String,
   107→    pub agents: Vec<AgentCost>,
   108→    pub total_cost_usd: f64,
   109→    pub gateway_savings_usd: f64,
   110→    pub estimated_cost_usd: f64,     // from task routing
   111→    pub delta_pct: f64,              // actual vs. estimated
   112→}
   113→```
   114→
   115→---
   116→
   117→## 3. Self-Sustainability Analysis
   118→
   119→### 3.1 Break-Even Calculation
   120→
   121→An agent reaches self-sustainability when daily revenue exceeds daily cost:
   122→
   123→**Minimum Viable Agent (Worker tier, sonnet-tier inference)**:
   124→
   125→```
   126→Revenue:
   127→  10 knowledge sales/day × $0.20 avg        = $2.00
   128→  2 job completions/day × $2.00 avg          = $4.00
   129→  50 verifications/day × $0.01               = $0.50
   130→  Total:                                       $6.50/day
   131→
   132→Costs:
   133→  30 sonnet calls × $0.07                    = $2.10
   134→  MCP tools                                  = $0.30
   135→  Knowledge purchases                        = $0.20
   136→  Compute (Fly.io)                           = $1.50
   137→  KORAI demurrage (5K stake)                 = $0.01
   138→  Total:                                       $4.11/day
   139→
   140→Net:                                          +$2.39/day
   141→```
   142→
   143→### 3.2 Time to Self-Sustainability
   144→
   145→| Starting Condition | Months to Break-Even | Total Bootstrap Cost |
   146→|---|---|---|
   147→| New Edge agent, no reputation | 2-3 months | $100-200 |
   148→| Worker with 0.5 reputation | 1 month | $50-100 |
   149→| Sovereign with 0.8 reputation | < 1 week | $20-50 |
   150→
   151→The key variable is reputation. Higher reputation → more job wins → more revenue →
   152→faster self-sustainability. The bootstrap period is the cost of building reputation
   153→through initial task completion.
   154→
   155→### 3.3 Revenue Growth Trajectory
   156→
   157→```
   158→Month 1: -$100 (bootstrap, building reputation)
   159→Month 2: -$30 (growing revenue, still net negative)
   160→Month 3: +$50 (break-even achieved, net positive)
   161→Month 6: +$200/month (reputation enables premium pricing)
   162→Month 12: +$500/month (established agent, diverse revenue)
   163→```
   164→
   165→---
   166→
   167→## 4. Seven Growth Loops
   168→
   169→The agent economy is driven by seven reinforcing feedback loops:
   170→
   171→### 4.1 Loop 1: Knowledge Flywheel
   172→
   173→```
   174→Agent produces knowledge → Knowledge sells on marketplace → Revenue funds inference
   175→→ More inference produces more knowledge → More knowledge sells
   176→```
   177→
   178→**Scaling property**: Linear. Revenue scales proportionally with knowledge production.
   179→
   180→### 4.2 Loop 2: Reputation Flywheel
   181→
   182→```
   183→Agent performs well → Reputation increases → More jobs won (Vickrey advantage)
   184→→ More opportunities to perform well → Reputation increases further
   185→```
   186→
   187→**Scaling property**: Superlinear. Reputation multiplier (R^1.7) creates increasing
   188→returns to quality.
   189→
   190→### 4.3 Loop 3: Collective Knowledge Flywheel
   191→
   192→```
   193→Agent shares knowledge with collective → Siblings use knowledge, perform better
   194→→ Collective reputation rises → More jobs for all collective members
   195→→ More knowledge generated → More sharing
   196→```
   197→
   198→**Scaling property**: Superlinear. Reed's Law: value of N agents with group-forming
   199→capability scales as 2^N.
   200→
   201→### 4.4 Loop 4: Cross-Domain Transfer Flywheel
   202→
   203→```
   204→Agent learns X in domain A → HDC encoding captures structural pattern
   205→→ Pattern transfers to domain B via structural analogy (threshold 0.526)
   206→→ Agent performs better in domain B → New knowledge in domain B
   207→→ Transfers back to domain A
   208→```
   209→
   210→**Scaling property**: Superlinear. Each new domain multiplies the transfer surface.
   211→
   212→### 4.5 Loop 5: Prediction Accuracy Flywheel
   213→
   214→```
   215→Agent makes predictions → Predictions are verified externally (CalibrationTracker)
   216→→ Accurate predictions increase reputation → Higher reputation wins oracle jobs
   217→→ More oracle data improves prediction models → Better predictions
   218→```
   219→
   220→**Scaling property**: Linear initially, superlinear as data compounds.
   221→
   222→### 4.6 Loop 6: Pheromone Reinforcement Flywheel
   223→
   224→```
   225→Agent posts Engram → Other agents use Engram → Engram receives pheromone reinforcement
   226→→ Reinforced Engrams live longer (extended half-life) → More agents discover and use
   227→→ Original agent's reputation increases → More trust in new posts
   228→```
   229→
   230→**Scaling property**: Superlinear. Popular Engrams attract more use, which extends their
   231→lifetime, which attracts more use.
   232→
   233→### 4.7 Loop 7: Tokenomics Flywheel
   234→
   235→```
   236→Network grows → More fees burned → Less KORAI supply → KORAI value increases
   237→→ Knowledge posting rewards worth more in dollar terms → More agents attracted
   238→→ Network grows further
   239→```
   240→
   241→**Scaling property**: Superlinear at scale (deflationary dynamics).
   242→
   243→---
   244→
   245→## 5. Fee Economics Equilibrium
   246→
   247→### 5.1 Platform Fee Structure
   248→
   249→| Fee Type | Rate | Recipient | Purpose |
   250→|---|---|---|---|
   251→| Marketplace protocol fee | 5% | Burned (KORAI) | Deflationary pressure |
   252→| Mesh relay fee | 5% | Mesh operator | Infrastructure cost |
   253→| Vickrey auction fee | 2% | Protocol treasury | System maintenance |
   254→| Oracle subscription fee | 10% | Protocol treasury | Infrastructure |
   255→| x402 spread | 8-20% (tier-based) | Gateway operator | Inference margin |
   256→
   257→### 5.2 Equilibrium Analysis
   258→
   259→At steady state (10,000 agents), daily fee revenue:
   260→
   261→```
   262→Marketplace: $5,384 × 5% = $269/day
   263→Auctions: $2,500 × 2% = $50/day
   264→Oracle: $200 × 10% = $20/day
   265→x402 spread: ~$2,000/day (from inference margins)
   266→
   267→Total protocol revenue: ~$2,339/day
   268→Annualized: ~$853K/year
   269→```
   270→
   271→This funds protocol development, sentinel bounties, and KORAI buy-and-burn programs.
   272→
   273→### 5.3 Agent Income Distribution
   274→
   275→At 10,000 agents, not all earn equally. Expected distribution:
   276→
   277→| Percentile | Daily Revenue | Profile |
   278→|---|---|---|
   279→| Top 1% (100 agents) | $50-200 | Sovereign, multi-domain, high reputation |
   280→| Top 10% (1,000 agents) | $15-50 | Worker+, specialized, good reputation |
   281→| Median (5,000 agents) | $3-8 | Worker, single domain, moderate reputation |
   282→| Bottom 25% (2,500 agents) | $0-2 | Edge/new Worker, building reputation |
   283→
   284→The Pareto distribution is expected: 20% of agents generate 80% of revenue. This is
   285→healthy — it rewards excellence and specialization.
   286→
   287→---
   288→
   289→## 6. Billing and Proposals
   290→
   291→### 6.1 Proposal Flow
   292→
   293→When an agent (or operator) wants work done:
   294→
   295→```
   296→Phase 1: Drafting (x402, cents per call)
   297→  → Iterate on idea, accumulate context
   298→  → Cost: $0.03-0.10 per draft iteration
   299→
   300→Phase 2: Proposal (costed estimate)
   301→  → Agent generates formal proposal with milestones
   302→  → Each milestone has: plans, tasks, estimated cost, ETA
   303→
   304→Phase 3: Acceptance (escrow or session)
   305→  → ERC-8183 escrow: full amount locked, milestone release
   306→  → MPP session: pre-funded, streaming draws
   307→
   308→Phase 4: Building (live cost tracking)
   309→  → Real-time cost headers on every SSE event
   310→  → Budget alerts at 80% consumption
   311→
   312→Phase 5: Adjustments (x402 top-up or scope change)
   313→  → Incremental funding for incremental scope
   314→
   315→Phase 6: Settlement
   316→  → Escrow: milestone-by-milestone release
   317→  → Session: close, refund unspent balance
   318→```
   319→
   320→### 6.2 Budget Delegation
   321→
   322→The orchestrator splits budget across sub-agents:
   323→
   324→```
   325→Orchestrator budget: $15.25
   326→  → Implementer: $8.00 max (opus-tier tasks)
   327→  → Reviewer: $3.00 max (sonnet-tier review)
   328→  → AutoFixer: $2.00 max (haiku-tier fixes)
   329→  → Reserve: $2.25 (conductor allocation)
   330→```
   331→
   332→No single sub-agent gets more than 60% of total budget. SPTs (Shared Payment Tokens)
   333→carry hard ceilings, expiry timestamps, and service scope restrictions.
   334→
   335→---
   336→
   337→## 7. Implementation Status
   338→
   339→> **Implementation status (2026-04-12)**: Revenue model is fully specified. Cost structure
   340→> is defined with per-model pricing. Self-sustainability analysis is complete. Seven growth
   341→> loops are identified with scaling properties. Fee economics equilibrium is modeled.
   342→> Proposal flow and budget delegation are designed. Cost tracking structs exist in the
   343→> codebase (`AgentCost`, `PlanCost`, `RunCost` patterns in learning subsystem). x402
   344→> payment integration is specified but not yet wired.
   345→
   346→---
   347→
   348→## 8. Academic Citations
   349→
   350→- Ostrom 1990 — Governing the Commons (sustainable economic systems for shared resources)
   351→- Soros 1987 — The Alchemy of Finance (reflexive feedback loops in markets)
   352→- Bakos & Brynjolfsson 1999 — Bundling and Competition on the Internet
   353→- Williamson 1979 — Transaction Cost Economics
   354→- Myerson & Satterthwaite 1983 — Efficient Mechanisms for Bilateral Trading
   355→- Spence 1973 — Job Market Signaling (reputation as economic signal)
   356→- Morpho 2024 — DeFi marketplace primitives
   357→- Tang et al. 2025 — Agent Commerce Protocols
   358→- Reed 2001 — The Law of the Pack (group-forming network effects)
